two-sum.speaker: luffy
two-sum.line: The merchant wants exactly 600,000 Berries for the map piece — not a coin more, not a coin less — and he only accepts TWO chests at once!

two-sum.speaker: nami
two-sum.line: There are only three chests here, so we could just try every pair by hand... but that gets ugly fast if there are hundreds of them.

two-sum.speaker: robin
two-sum.line: Then let's not check every pair. I'll remember every chest we've already opened — and for each new one, I only need to ask: 'have I already seen the amount that completes it?'

two-sum.speaker: robin
two-sum.line: Chest one: 300,000. I need 300,000 more to reach the target — nothing in my notes yet. I'll log it and move on.

two-sum.speaker: robin
two-sum.line: Chest two: 200,000. I need 400,000 more — still nothing logged that matches. Log this one too.

two-sum.speaker: robin
two-sum.line: Chest three: 400,000. I need 200,000 more to complete it — and that's already in my notes, from chest two!

two-sum.speaker: nami
two-sum.line: Chest two and chest three — 200,000 plus 400,000 is exactly 600,000. That's the pair.

two-sum.speaker: luffy
two-sum.line: SHISHISHI! And you never even touched chest one again! Meat time!

two-sum.speaker: robin
two-sum.line: That's the whole trick: check the notes before you write anything new down, and you only ever look at each chest once. One pass, one pair — never re-comparing chests against each other directly.

two-sum-ii.speaker: usopp
two-sum-ii.line: Iceburg's blueprint says the crossbeam brace needs two salvaged plates that weigh EXACTLY 25 stone together. Over or under and the whole frame's stress rating is wrong.

two-sum-ii.speaker: nami
two-sum-ii.line: Six plates on the rack, already sorted lightest to heaviest. We are NOT weighing every possible pair — that's fifteen trips up this ladder.

two-sum-ii.speaker: nami
two-sum-ii.line: Lightest plate and heaviest plate, together: 4 plus 20 is 24. One short. The only honest fix is the light end giving us more.

two-sum-ii.speaker: usopp
two-sum-ii.line: Then I step in from the light end — plate 7 instead of plate 4.

two-sum-ii.speaker: nami
two-sum-ii.line: 7 plus 20 is 27 — now we're over. Heavy end steps down.

two-sum-ii.speaker: usopp
two-sum-ii.line: 7 plus 16 is 23 — short again. Light end steps in once more, to plate 9.

two-sum-ii.speaker: nami
two-sum-ii.line: 9 plus 16 is 25. Exact. Plates three and five come off the rack.

two-sum-ii.speaker: usopp
two-sum-ii.line: And plate 4, plate 20 — never touched again after we stepped past them. Once an end steps inward, it never steps back out. That's the whole trick, Iceburg-san!

longest-substring.speaker: brook
longest-substring.line: Fifty years alone on the Rumbar's deck, and I only had this much of the melody left in my head — I promised Laboon I'd play the whole thing someday. Yohoho.

longest-substring.speaker: chopper
longest-substring.line: But some of the notes repeat! If I hum a note twice in the same phrase it sounds muddy — you said so yourself. What's the LONGEST clean run in here, with no note repeated?

longest-substring.speaker: brook
longest-substring.line: First note: B. A phrase of one, and it's clean so far.

longest-substring.speaker: brook
longest-substring.line: I, then N, then K — none of them repeat anything still ringing in the phrase. The window just keeps growing to the right.

longest-substring.speaker: chopper
longest-substring.line: Next note's a B again — and we already have one ringing, back at the start of the phrase!

longest-substring.speaker: brook
longest-substring.line: So the phrase can no longer start before the note right after that old B. Drop it from the front — the window's left edge jumps forward, not one note at a time, straight to where it needs to be.

longest-substring.speaker: brook
longest-substring.line: Last note: S. Nothing in the current phrase repeats it — the window grows again, and this is the longest clean run yet.

longest-substring.speaker: chopper
longest-substring.line: Five notes, never checking a note twice against the whole phrase — just remembering the last place you saw each one. That's amazing, Brook!

linked-list-cycle.speaker: luffy
linked-list-cycle.line: Ships go into the Florian Triangle's fog and just... never come back out, right? Let's find out if our OWN charted course is one of those loops before we sail it for real.

linked-list-cycle.speaker: chopper
linked-list-cycle.line: I'll walk the route at normal speed, one waypoint per minute. You go Gear Second — two waypoints a minute. If the course loops, you'll catch up to me eventually. If it doesn't, you just sail off the edge of the chart and we're done.

linked-list-cycle.speaker: luffy
linked-list-cycle.line: One minute in: I'm already two waypoints ahead, at C. You're only at B.

linked-list-cycle.speaker: chopper
linked-list-cycle.line: Two minutes: I've reached C. You've raced ahead to E — through D. If this route were a straight line to open sea, you'd just keep pulling away from me forever.

linked-list-cycle.speaker: luffy
linked-list-cycle.line: But it's NOT a straight line — past E, the fog loops back to C! So my next two steps put me right back at D... and you're walking into D too!

linked-list-cycle.speaker: chopper
linked-list-cycle.line: We're standing on the same waypoint. The fast route lapped the slow one — that ONLY happens if the course loops. It's the Florian Triangle, alright. We chart around it.

linked-list-cycle.speaker: luffy
linked-list-cycle.line: SHISHISHI, no ghost ship's getting the Sunny! And I didn't even need a map of every waypoint we'd already visited — just two speeds!

merge-intervals-ep.speaker: zoro
merge-intervals-ep.line: We stole Baroque Works' patrol logs. Four watch shifts on the palace wall, sorted by the hour each one starts. We need to get Vivi's group in through a gap in the watch — not fight through it.

merge-intervals-ep.speaker: nami
merge-intervals-ep.line: Next shift starts at hour 4 — before the first one even ends at 5. That's not a second watch, that's the SAME watch overlapping. Merge them: 2 to 8.

merge-intervals-ep.speaker: zoro
merge-intervals-ep.line: Hour 11 starts after hour 8 — nobody's watching in between. New block.

merge-intervals-ep.speaker: nami
merge-intervals-ep.line: But hour 12 overlaps THAT one before it's done at 13. Merge again: 11 to 16.

merge-intervals-ep.speaker: nami
merge-intervals-ep.line: Two guarded stretches, start to finish: 2 to 8, and 11 to 16. Everything else — including the three hours between 8 and 11 — is open air.

merge-intervals-ep.speaker: zoro
merge-intervals-ep.line: We didn't need to know who was on which shift, or when any ONE guard blinks. Just where the merged blocks end — and where they don't reach. That's our window.

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: robin
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: The old dynasty stored these scrolls by year, earliest to latest. Then the tremor spun this shelf — it's no longer sorted start to end, but it's not random either. We want the scroll marked 20.

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: luffy
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: So how do we search a sorted shelf that isn't sorted anymore?!

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: robin
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: We still check the middle first. Scroll three: '10'.

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: robin
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: The FIRST scroll reads '50', bigger than the middle one — that proves the left side is the half that got wrapped around. The right side, 10 through 40, is still in honest year order, and 20 falls inside that range. So we hunt to the right.

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: robin
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: Middle of what's left: scroll five, '30'. Our target is smaller, and this little stretch is still sorted — so 20 has to sit before it.

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: robin
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: Which leaves exactly one scroll. Scroll four: '20'. Found.

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: luffy
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: Three checks for seven scrolls! ...but how did you know WHICH half was the honest one?

search-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: robin
search-rotated-sorted-array.line: A single rotation only ever breaks the order in one place. So at every split, at least one half is still perfectly sorted — I just compare its two ends to know which one to trust, and rule out the other half entirely.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: LADIES AND GENTLEMEN! Corrida Colosseum block battle undercard! Bring me every gladiator's power reading as they step in, one at a time, and I've gotta tell the crowd: how many MORE entrances until somebody finally tops THIS one?!

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: First in: power 73. Nobody's beaten him yet — he goes on my 'still waiting' list, on top.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: Power 74 steps in — stronger than 73! That settles it: gladiator one is topped after exactly ONE more entrance. He's off my list; 74 takes his spot.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: 75 tops 74 — same story, ONE entrance later. 75 takes the spot on my list now.

daily-temperatures.speaker: luffy
daily-temperatures.line: 71's nowhere near strong enough to touch 75!

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: Right — so he doesn't knock anyone out. He just joins the waiting list BEHIND 75. Both stay up, and my list is no longer just one name — it's ordered strongest-first.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: 69's weaker still — joins the back of the list too. Three unresolved gladiators now, strictly getting weaker toward the back.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: Now 72 walks in. He beats 69 at the BACK of my list — one entrance, resolved. Then he beats 71 too — that one waited TWO entrances. But he does NOT beat 75, still standing at the front. Two knockouts, one arrival.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: 76 flattens what's left of my list — beats 72 (one entrance) AND beats 75, who'd been waiting since gladiator two — FOUR entrances. The whole list is empty. 76 stands alone.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: Last one in: 73. Doesn't beat 76. Joins the list — and the show's over before anyone tops him.

daily-temperatures.speaker: usopp
daily-temperatures.line: Gladiator six and seven — 76 and 73 — nobody ever showed up to beat them. Their wait stays 0. And notice: I never once rewound the tape to recheck an earlier fighter. Every gladiator gets resolved the SECOND somebody stronger walks in — or never.

daily-temperatures.speaker: luffy
daily-temperatures.line: SHISHISHI, so the 'wait' list only ever GROWS at the back and gets chopped from the top — you never have to go digging through the middle!

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: chopper
next-greater-element-ii.line: Big Mom's tea table is ROUND — five treats, tier heights 5, 3, 8, 3, 6, going clockwise from the top. For each treat, what's the next TALLER one clockwise? And since the table's a circle, 'next' is allowed to wrap all the way back around to the start!

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: robin
next-greater-element-ii.line: First treat: 5 layers. Nothing taller has shown up yet — it just waits.

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: robin
next-greater-element-ii.line: Next, clockwise: 3 layers. Shorter than 5 — it just joins behind it on the wait list.

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: robin
next-greater-element-ii.line: Third treat: 8 layers — taller than the 3 right behind it in line, AND taller than the 5 before that. Both resolve at once, answer 8 for each. The wait list is empty again.

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: robin
next-greater-element-ii.line: Fourth: 3 layers again. Shorter than 8 — waits.

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: robin
next-greater-element-ii.line: Fifth: 6 layers. Taller than the 3 waiting behind it — that one resolves, answer 6. But NOT taller than the 8 still standing before it, so that one keeps waiting.

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: chopper
next-greater-element-ii.line: We're back at the top of the table — but the 8-layer treat is STILL undefeated, and the 6-layer one is still waiting behind it. Since it's round, we just... keep going?

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: robin
next-greater-element-ii.line: Exactly — one more lap. Passing the 5-layer and 3-layer treats again changes nothing, they're already resolved. But passing the 8-layer treat a SECOND time — it finally tops the 6-layer treat still waiting behind it. Found by wrapping around.

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: robin
next-greater-element-ii.line: One treat never gets topped, even after two full laps — the 8-layer one itself. Its answer stays 'none.' And we only ever circled the table TWICE total, not once per treat — still O(n), not O(n²).

next-greater-element-ii.speaker: luffy
next-greater-element-ii.line: So a round table just means: walk it twice instead of once, but the stack doesn't care that it's round at all!

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: usopp
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: Dock One's got six stacks of salvaged lumber, different heights, lined up in a row. Iceburg wants ONE tarp — a single rectangle — laid across as many CONSECUTIVE stacks as possible. Snag is, the tarp can only stand as tall as the SHORTEST stack it covers, or it sags into the gap.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: chopper
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: First stack: 2 feet. Nothing to compare yet — just note it and keep going.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: chopper
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: Second stack: only 1 foot — shorter than the first! That means the 2-foot stack can NEVER anchor a tarp past this point. Close it out right now: 2 feet tall, exactly 1 stack wide. 2 square feet — our best so far.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: chopper
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: Third: 5 feet — taller than the 1-foot stack waiting. No closing yet, just stack it onto the wait list.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: chopper
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: Fourth: 6 feet, taller still. The wait list keeps growing — strictly increasing heights, bottom to top.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: chopper
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: Fifth: only 2 feet. Shorter than BOTH the 6-footer and the 5-footer. First, the 6-foot stack closes out — by itself, just 1 stack wide: 6 square feet. Then the 5-foot stack closes out too — and now it stretches across where the 6-footer USED to stand: 5 tall, 2 wide, 10 square feet!

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: chopper
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: Sixth and last: 3 feet, taller than what's waiting. Stack it — nothing closes yet.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: usopp
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: No more stacks coming in — but three are STILL sitting on the wait list, and every single one still needs an answer. Close them out, right to left.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: chopper
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: The 3-footer only ever had 1 stack under it: 3 square feet. The 2-footer, once the 3-foot stack is gone, actually spans FOUR stacks: 2 times 4 is 8. And the very FIRST stack we ever placed — 1 foot tall — once everything taller is cleared, spans the whole dock: all 6 stacks, 6 square feet.

largest-rectangle-histogram.speaker: usopp
largest-rectangle-histogram.line: Biggest tarp on the whole dock: still 10 square feet, from stacks three and four. And that flush-at-the-end step — closing out whoever's LEFT once the stacks run out — is the part everybody forgets. The wait list doesn't resolve itself just because there's nothing new arriving.

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Every day of this voyage, I log the day's total haul — and I want to know, the SECOND I write it down, how many days in a row, counting backward from today, were all worth LESS than or equal to today. No peeking ahead — I only know today's number when I log it.

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Day 0: 100k. First entry, nothing behind it to compare. Span: 1.

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Day 1: 80k. Worse than yesterday — can't absorb anything behind it. Span: 1, on its own.

online-stock-span.speaker: luffy
online-stock-span.line: That's a bad haul day, Nami!

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Day 2: 60k. Even worse. Span: 1 again — three straight days now that just sit on the ledger, none of them beating the one before.

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Day 3: 70k — beats yesterday's 60k! I don't just say 'span 1' — I ABSORB yesterday's whole span into mine: 1 (mine) plus 1 (theirs) is 2. But 70 does NOT beat day 1's 80k, so I stop absorbing there.

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Day 4: 60k again — worse than yesterday's 70k. Can't absorb anything. Back down to span 1.

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Day 5: 75k. Beats yesterday's 60k — absorb its span of 1. THEN beats day 3's 70k too — and that one's span was already 2, since it had already absorbed day 2! Absorb that whole chain: 1 plus 1 plus 2 is 4. Stops there — 75 doesn't beat day 1's 80k.

online-stock-span.speaker: nami
online-stock-span.line: Day 6: 85k — our best haul yet! Beats yesterday's 75k, absorbing its span of 4. THEN beats day 1's 80k too, absorbing its span of 1. Total: 1 plus 4 plus 1 is 6 — six days running, all at or below today, every single one of them chained together without ever re-reading a single day's price twice.

online-stock-span.speaker: luffy
online-stock-span.line: Only day 0's 100k is still standing above us! SHISHISHI, we're on a hot streak, Nami!

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: luffy
range-sum-query-immutable.line: Nami, how far did we sail between the crab island and the singing cliffs?

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: nami
range-sum-query-immutable.line: Hang on — before I answer that by hand, I'm writing down a running total for the WHOLE voyage, once. Day 0: 5 miles. Running total after day 0: 5.

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: nami
range-sum-query-immutable.line: Day 1: 3 more miles. I don't just note '3' — I write the NEW total: 5 plus 3 is 8. Day 2: 8 more, total 16.

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: nami
range-sum-query-immutable.line: Day 3: 2 more, total 18. Day 4: 6 more, total 24. Day 5: 4 more, total 28. Ledger's done — built ONCE, in a single pass down the whole voyage.

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: nami
range-sum-query-immutable.line: So: how far from day 1 to day 3? I don't re-add a single mile. Running total AFTER day 3, minus running total BEFORE day 1 — which is just the total after day 0. 18 minus 5 is 13.

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: luffy
range-sum-query-immutable.line: Ooh — what about day 2 through day 5?!

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: nami
range-sum-query-immutable.line: Same trick: total after day 5 minus total after day 1. 28 minus 8 is 20. I could answer a THOUSAND more of these and never touch the raw distances again.

range-sum-query-immutable.speaker: luffy
range-sum-query-immutable.line: SHISHISHI, you only walked the voyage once, and now you can answer forever!

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: This corridor's wall is carved with a running sequence of numbers — some marked with a subtractive stroke. I want every stretch of CONSECUTIVE glyphs that adds to exactly 5, this shrine's sacred number.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: Before reading a single glyph, I log one thing for free: a running total of 0, meaning 'nothing read yet.' That's what lets a stretch starting at the very FIRST glyph get counted too.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: First glyph: +2. Running total: 2. To close a stretch HERE, I'd need to have already logged a total of 2 minus 5 — negative 3. I haven't. Log today's total and move on.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: Second glyph: +3. Running total: 5. I need a PREVIOUS total of 5 minus 5 — zero. And I logged exactly that, before I'd even read a glyph! Glyphs one and two together sum to 5. First stretch found.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: chopper
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: You never rescanned! You just asked your notes if you'd already seen that number!

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: Third glyph: -2, the subtractive mark. Running total: 3. Need 3 minus 5 — negative 2. Not logged. Log 3 and continue.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: Fourth glyph: +5, on its own. Running total: 8. Need 8 minus 5 — three. I logged THAT one just now, from glyph three! So glyph four, all by itself, is a stretch that sums to 5.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: Fifth: +1, total 9, need 4 — not logged. Sixth: -3, total 6, need 1 — not logged. Log both and keep walking.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: Seventh, last glyph: +4. Running total: 10. Need 10 minus 5 — five. And I logged THAT one all the way back after glyph two! So glyphs THREE through SEVEN — five glyphs, subtractive marks and all — also sum to 5.

subarray-sum-equals-k.speaker: robin
subarray-sum-equals-k.line: Three stretches — a pair, a lone glyph, and a five-glyph run — found in one walk down the wall. The number I ever needed to have already seen was always today's total minus 5.

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: usopp
continuous-subarray-sum.line: The Going Merry's booster tank holds fuel in units of 8 — one full cycle. I log units burned every day. Is there ever a stretch of AT LEAST TWO consecutive days that burns an exact multiple of 8 — clean cycles, zero left over?

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: usopp
continuous-subarray-sum.line: Day 0: burned 9. Running total: 9. Here's the trick — I don't care about the total itself, only the REMAINDER after dividing by 8: 9 mod 8 is 1. Never seen remainder 1 — log it, tagged to day 0.

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: usopp
continuous-subarray-sum.line: Day 1: 3 more, total 12. Remainder: 12 mod 8 is 4. New — log it. Day 2: 6 more, total 18. Remainder: 18 mod 8 is 2. Also new — log it.

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: usopp
continuous-subarray-sum.line: Day 3: burned 8 more — a FULL tank, all by itself! Running total 26. Remainder: 26 mod 8 is 2. I've SEEN that remainder — at day 2! But day 2 to day 3 is only ONE day apart.

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: chopper
continuous-subarray-sum.line: But day 3 burned EXACTLY 8 — a whole tank! Doesn't that count?!

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: usopp
continuous-subarray-sum.line: Not on its own — the rule needs a STRETCH of at least two days working together, not one lucky day. I keep the OLDER day-2 remainder logged, and I do NOT count this one.

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: usopp
continuous-subarray-sum.line: Day 4: 2 more, total 28. Remainder: 28 mod 8 is 4 — and THAT remainder was logged all the way back at day 1! Days 2 through 4: three whole days, burning 6 plus 8 plus 2, sixteen units — exactly two full tanks.

continuous-subarray-sum.speaker: usopp
continuous-subarray-sum.line: That's the one everybody forgets — checking the gap length BEFORE celebrating a remainder match. A lone perfect day doesn't prove a cycle. A stretch does.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: The Gate of Justice control panel — four dials. CP9 designed it so no single dial's correct setting can be read off directly: each dial's true value has to equal the PRODUCT of all three OTHER dials.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: zoro
product-of-array-except-self.line: So multiply the other three together for each dial. What's the problem?

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: One dial currently reads zero. The lazy shortcut — multiply ALL four dials, then divide by each one — breaks the instant you divide by that zero. We need a way that never divides at all.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: First sweep, left to right: a running product of everything BEFORE each dial. Nothing sits before dial one — its 'before' product is 1. Carry dial one's own value, 4, forward for whoever's next.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: Dial two: everything before it is just dial one — 'before' product 4. Dial three: everything before it is dial one and two — 4 times 3 is 12.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: Dial four: everything before it includes the zero dial — so its 'before' product is 0. That's not a mistake to fix — dial three genuinely erases everything that comes after it.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: Second sweep, right to left: a running product of everything AFTER each dial, combined with what we already have. Dial four: nothing after it — combine with 1. Its answer stays 0.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: Dial three: everything after it is just dial four, value 5. Combine: 12 times 5 is 60. Dial three's true setting is 60.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: Dial two: everything after it includes dial three's zero — 4 times 0 is 0. Dial one: everything after it also includes that same zero — 1 times 0 is 0.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: robin
product-of-array-except-self.line: Final dial: 0 too, for the same reason. Three of the four true settings are zero, and only dial three actually matters — and we never divided by the zero dial that would have crashed the whole calculation.

product-of-array-except-self.speaker: zoro
product-of-array-except-self.line: Two clean sweeps. Left to right, then right to left. No division anywhere. Let's move.

reverse-linked-list.speaker: usopp
reverse-linked-list.line: Picture it: a shipwreck survivor's escape trail — sign one points to sign two, sign two points to sign three, and sign three points at nothing, because that is where the trail used to end!

reverse-linked-list.speaker: nami
reverse-linked-list.line: Problem is, we are leaving FROM sign three. If we want a trail that guides someone back the way we came, every single arrow needs to point the opposite direction — and we can only carry a couple of signs in our hands at once, not the whole trail.

reverse-linked-list.speaker: zoro
reverse-linked-list.line: Wrong way is basically my specialty. Let's go.

reverse-linked-list.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list.line: Three hands is all it takes. One hand — prev — holds the last sign we already flipped, empty at the start. One hand — curr — holds the sign we are working on right now, starting at sign one. And before we flip curr's arrow, we write down where it used to point, in a third hand, next — or we would lose the rest of the trail forever.

reverse-linked-list.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list.line: Sign one: I note that it used to point to sign two — that's next. Now I flip sign one's arrow to point at prev, which is nothing yet. Prev becomes sign one. Curr moves up to sign two.

reverse-linked-list.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list.line: Sign two: next is sign three. Flip sign two's arrow back to point at prev, sign one. Prev becomes sign two, curr moves up to sign three.

reverse-linked-list.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list.line: Sign three: next is nothing — the old end of the trail. Flip sign three's arrow back to point at prev, sign two. Prev becomes sign three, and curr runs off the end. We're out of signs to flip.

reverse-linked-list.speaker: luffy
reverse-linked-list.line: SHISHISHI! Now sign three points to two, two points to one, and one points at nothing — the trail runs the exact opposite way!

reverse-linked-list.speaker: zoro
reverse-linked-list.line: Told you. Every trail I walk ends up backward eventually.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: usopp
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: Same trail, five signs this time: one through five. But the merchant only wants the MIDDLE stretch flipped — signs two through four. Signs one and five stay exactly as they are.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: nami
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: And we still only get one walk down the trail — no rebuilding it from scratch, no spare pile of signs on the side.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: First, walk to the sign just before position two — that's sign one. Call it the anchor. Everything after the anchor is the stretch we're allowed to touch.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: Curr starts at sign two — it will end up as the TAIL of the flipped stretch, so it stays put the whole time. Each round, I grab whatever sign currently sits right after curr, and re-hang it directly after the anchor instead.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: Round one: the sign right after curr is three. Pull it out, hang it right after the anchor. Trail now reads one, three, two, four, five.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: Round two: curr — still sign two — now has sign four sitting right after it. Pull four out, hang it right after the anchor too. Trail reads one, four, three, two, five.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: That's right minus left — two rounds — and positions two through four are fully flipped. I never touched sign one or sign five.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: chopper
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: So sign one and sign five never even noticed anything happened?

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: robin
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: Exactly. That's the whole point of stopping the anchor exactly one sign before the range — everything outside it just stays wired to whatever the range's new ends turn out to be.

reverse-linked-list-ii.speaker: luffy
reverse-linked-list-ii.line: SHISHISHI, half a trail flipped and nobody had to redraw the whole map!

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: luffy
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: Four crew members lined up for the relay, one through four! Vivi says we gotta swap every NEIGHBORING pair — but only by re-tying the baton cords, no starting the line over from scratch!

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: nami
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: So runner one and two trade spots, then runner three and four trade spots. If there's an odd runner left alone at the very end with no partner, they just stay put.

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: robin
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: Same trick as always — an anchor standing just before the current pair. Point the anchor at the SECOND runner, point the first runner at whatever came after the second, then point the second runner back at the first. That's the whole swap.

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: robin
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: Pair one done: the anchor now points to runner two, runner two points to runner one, and runner one points to whatever comes next — runner three. The line reads two, one, three, four so far.

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: robin
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: Move the anchor up to runner one, since that pair is finished. Next pair: three and four.

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: robin
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: Pair two done: runner one now points to runner four, runner four points to runner three, and runner three points to nothing — the end of the line. Final order: two, one, four, three.

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: chopper
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: We never had to write down a whole new list — we just kept re-tying three cords at a time?

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: robin
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: Exactly — one anchor, one pair, three arrows rewired, every round. Constant extra space.

swap-nodes-in-pairs.speaker: zoro
swap-nodes-in-pairs.line: Fine. Just don't make me swap with Sanji.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: usopp
sliding-window-maximum.line: Eight wave-height readings roll in off the bow: one, three, minus one, minus three, five, three, six, seven! Nami wants the TALLEST wave in every stretch of three consecutive readings, sliding one at a time, all the way down the line!

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: nami
sliding-window-maximum.line: Checking all three readings by hand for every single window works, but with eight numbers that's already six windows re-scanned from scratch. On a real logbook with thousands of readings, that's far too slow.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: We don't need to re-scan. Keep a lineup of candidate wave heights, largest to smallest, front to back — the moment a new reading beats whoever's waiting at the back, those smaller ones get tossed immediately, since they can never win a window while a taller wave still stands in front of them. And before reading off the front as our answer, we always check whether that front reading has aged out of the current window entirely.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading one: height one. Lineup is empty, so it just joins: [1].

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading two: height three. Bigger than the one at the back of the lineup, so one gets tossed out first — then three joins alone: [3].

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading three: minus one. Smaller than the back of the lineup, so it just joins behind: [3, -1]. Now three readings are in view — window complete. The tallest is whoever's at the very front: three.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading four: minus three. Also smaller than the back, joins behind: [3, -1, -3]. Front is still three, and three is still inside our three-wide window, so the answer stays three.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading five: height five! Bigger than everyone currently waiting, so minus three, minus one, AND three all get tossed — five stands alone: [5]. New tallest: five.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading six: height three. Smaller than the back (five), so it just joins behind: [5, 3]. Front is still five — still tallest.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading seven: height six. Bigger than three AND five, so both get tossed — six stands alone: [6].

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: robin
sliding-window-maximum.line: Reading eight, the last one: height seven. Bigger than six, tosses it too — seven stands alone: [7]. Tallest of the final window: seven.

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: luffy
sliding-window-maximum.line: SHISHISHI! Every window's tallest wave, and nobody ever had to look backward — only forward, tossing the little ones overboard!

sliding-window-maximum.speaker: chopper
sliding-window-maximum.line: And a wave only ever gets tossed out once, right? So it's not nearly as much work as it looked at first!

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: chopper
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: New vitals reading every few seconds — but a single reading jumps all over the place! I only want the average of the LAST three readings, updated every time a new one comes in.

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: nami
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: Recomputing the sum of the last three from scratch every single time works, but on a long stream that's a lot of repeated adding for numbers we already added before.

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: robin
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: So don't repeat it. Keep a small line of at most three readings, plus a running total. Add the new reading to the total and to the back of the line — and the moment the line grows past three, drop the oldest one off the front, subtracting it from the total too.

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: robin
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: First reading: one. Line: [1]. Total: one. Average of one reading: 1.0.

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: robin
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: Second reading: ten. Line: [1, 10]. Total: eleven. Average of two readings: 5.5.

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: robin
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: Third reading: three. Line: [1, 10, 3] — full, exactly three readings. Total: fourteen. Average: about 4.67.

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: robin
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: Fourth reading: five. The line is already full, so before adding five, drop the oldest reading — one — off the front, and subtract it from the total. New total: thirteen. Now add five: line [10, 3, 5], total eighteen.

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: luffy
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: SHISHISHI! Average of the last three, every time, and you never re-added the same number twice!

moving-average-from-data-stream.speaker: chopper
moving-average-from-data-stream.line: That's exactly the smoothing I needed — one running total, one small line, done.

design-circular-deque.speaker: usopp
design-circular-deque.line: Behold! The Legendary Three-Peg Rum Barrel of Baratie! Exactly three pegs, arranged in a ring — sacks can be hung from the FRONT peg or the BACK peg, and pulled off from either end too. But there is no fourth peg. Ever.

design-circular-deque.speaker: nami
design-circular-deque.line: And we are not shifting every sack over each time something changes — the barrel just tracks which peg is currently the front and which is currently the rear, and walks around the ring.

design-circular-deque.speaker: robin
design-circular-deque.line: insertLast, one: hang sack one on an empty peg, and mark it BOTH the front and the rear, since it's the only sack in the barrel.

design-circular-deque.speaker: robin
design-circular-deque.line: insertLast, two: hang sack two on the next peg around, and move the rear marker up to it.

design-circular-deque.speaker: robin
design-circular-deque.line: insertFront, three: hang sack three on the peg just BEFORE the current front, and move the front marker back to it. Now all three pegs are full: three, one, two.

design-circular-deque.speaker: robin
design-circular-deque.line: insertFront, four: every peg is already hung. There's nowhere to put it — return false, and the barrel doesn't change at all.

design-circular-deque.speaker: usopp
design-circular-deque.line: getRear! The peg marked rear is holding sack two — so getRear returns two, no lifting required, just a peek!

design-circular-deque.speaker: nami
design-circular-deque.line: isFull, checked: three pegs, three sacks. True.

design-circular-deque.speaker: robin
design-circular-deque.line: deleteLast: lift sack two off the rear peg, and move the rear marker back one peg, to sack one. The barrel now holds three, one — one peg free again.

design-circular-deque.speaker: robin
design-circular-deque.line: insertFront, four again: now there IS a free peg, just before the current front. Hang four there, and move the front marker to it. Barrel: four, three, one.

design-circular-deque.speaker: brook
design-circular-deque.line: Yohohoho! And getFront now returns four, the newest sack at the front peg — though I have no lips left to whistle at how tidy that ring buffer is.

design-circular-deque.speaker: zoro
design-circular-deque.line: No shifting, no new barrel. Just walk the ring.

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: usopp
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: The Marine watchtower has three floors of guards: the commander alone at the top — room three. Below, two lieutenants — rooms nine and twenty. Below THEM, two grunts — rooms fifteen and seven, both guarding under room twenty.

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: nami
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: We are not sneaking straight down to the grunts. We clear a floor COMPLETELY before descending to the next one — record every room on this floor, then move to their doors.

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: robin
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: That's a queue, not a stack. Push the root in first: room three. Queue: [3].

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: robin
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: Before draining, I snapshot how many rooms are on this floor — right now, just one. Pop room three, record it as this level's list, and push its two children, nine and twenty, onto the back of the queue.

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: robin
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: New floor, new snapshot: two rooms waiting. Pop room nine — it has no children, nothing to push. Pop room twenty — push ITS two children, fifteen and seven, onto the back.

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: robin
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: Final floor: two rooms, fifteen and seven. Pop both, neither has children, nothing more gets pushed. Queue is empty — we've cleared every floor.

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: chopper
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: So the snapshot number is what keeps us from mixing a grunt from THIS floor in with a lieutenant from the last one?

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: robin
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: Exactly — grab that count once, drain exactly that many before you check the count again. New children pushed mid-floor never get counted until the NEXT floor's snapshot.

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.speaker: luffy
binary-tree-level-order-traversal.line: SHISHISHI! Top floor first, then both floors under it, together, never skipping ahead! Now let's go get the commander!

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: chopper
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: This ruin has branching tunnels — from the entrance, LEFT goes to a passage, RIGHT goes to another. I want to know how many tunnel-lengths deep the FARTHEST dead end goes.

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: Ask each branch how deep IT goes, and trust the answer. A tunnel with no branches past it is one level deep, all by itself.

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: Left branch from the entrance, room nine: no further branches. Depth one.

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: Right branch from the entrance, room twenty: not a dead end — it splits again into room fifteen and room seven. Ask THEM first.

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: Room fifteen: no further branches. Depth one. Room seven: same, no further branches. Depth one.

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: So room twenty's depth is one MORE than the deeper of its two children — both are depth one, so room twenty is depth two.

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: Back at the entrance: left branch was depth one, right branch was depth two. The entrance's own depth is one more than the DEEPER of the two — one more than two.

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: luffy
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: SHISHISHI! Three tunnel-lengths deep, straight down through room twenty and room fifteen or room seven! Let's go find treasure!

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.speaker: zoro
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.line: Every dead end reports back up. No need to check the whole map at once.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: nami
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: Five crewmates are posted at lookout points, connected like a family tree: one at the center, two and three branching off from one, and four and five branching off from two. I want the LONGEST rope bridge connecting any two of them — and it doesn't have to pass through the center.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: brook
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: Yohohoho, a bridge that may skip the captain entirely? How delightfully mutinous.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: Measure it from the bottom up. At every crewmate, ask: how tall is my left branch, and how tall is my right branch? The longest bridge crossing through ME is left-height plus right-height — track the biggest one we see anywhere in the tree, not just at the center.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: Crewmate four: no branches at all. Height one. Crewmate five: same, height one. Neither contributes a bridge on their own.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: Crewmate two: left branch height one (crewmate four), right branch height one (crewmate five). A bridge through crewmate two spans one plus one — two rope-lengths. That's our best so far. Crewmate two's own height is one more than the taller branch: two.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: Crewmate three: no branches. Height one. No bridge to track there.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: robin
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: The center, crewmate one: left branch height two (crewmate two's whole side), right branch height one (crewmate three). A bridge through the center spans two plus one — THREE rope-lengths. That beats our previous best of two.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: nami
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: Three rope-lengths, running from crewmate four or five, through crewmate two, through the center, out to crewmate three. And it never mattered that the center was involved — the math treated it the same as any other post.

diameter-of-binary-tree.speaker: luffy
diameter-of-binary-tree.line: SHISHISHI! Longest bridge on the whole island, and we found it just by asking every post how tall its own branches were!

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: usopp
validate-binary-search-tree.line: Sengoku found this filed as a proper Bounty Ledger — a search tree, sorted so anyone can find a name by going left for smaller, right for bigger! But is it REAL, or forged?

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: robin
validate-binary-search-tree.line: The rule for a real one is strict: at EVERY node, everything in its left branch must be smaller, and everything in its right branch must be bigger — no matter how many levels down.

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: chopper
validate-binary-search-tree.line: Can't we just check each entry against its own parent? That seems like less work.

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: robin
validate-binary-search-tree.line: That's the trap. A node has to obey EVERY ancestor above it, not just the one directly above. So as I go down, I'll carry a valid range — a floor and a ceiling — that narrows at each branch.

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: robin
validate-binary-search-tree.line: Root: 10, range is wide open. Fine. Its left branch must now stay below 10; its right branch must stay above 10.

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: robin
validate-binary-search-tree.line: Left to 5, range (-inf, 10): 5 fits, no children to check. That whole branch is clean.

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: robin
validate-binary-search-tree.line: Right to 15, range (10, inf): 15 fits. Its left child now inherits BOTH bounds — must stay above 10 (from the root) AND below 15 (from its own parent).

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: robin
validate-binary-search-tree.line: 15's left child is 6. Range required: (10, 15). 6 is below 15, sure — but it is NOT above 10. Forged.

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: nami
validate-binary-search-tree.line: And there's the con — if we'd only compared 6 to its direct parent, 15, it would've looked perfectly fine. The root's rule has to follow every entry all the way down.

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: luffy
validate-binary-search-tree.line: SHISHISHI! One sneaky little 6 stuffed two levels deep, and the WHOLE ledger's a fake!

validate-binary-search-tree.speaker: zoro
validate-binary-search-tree.line: Answer: false. We didn't even need to check 20 once the left side broke it.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: usopp
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: Two scouts are posted on this sorted lookout tree — one at value 3, one at value 5. Command wants the lowest post that watches over BOTH of them.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: nami
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: The safe way: walk from the root all the way down to 3, walk from the root all the way down to 5, write down both full paths, then compare them to see where they last agree. Works, but it's two walks and two lists.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: robin
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: We can do it in ONE walk, because this is a search tree. At each post, compare both 3 and 5 to the post's value: if BOTH are smaller, the answer is further left. If BOTH are bigger, further right. The moment they land on different sides, that post IS the answer.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: robin
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: Start at 6: is 3 < 6? Yes. Is 5 < 6? Yes. Both smaller — go left.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: robin
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: At 2: is 3 < 2? No. Is 5 < 2? No. Both are BIGGER than 2 — go right.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: robin
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: At 4: 3 is smaller than 4, but 5 is bigger than 4. They finally split! This is the deepest post where the two trails part ways — so 4 is the lowest common ancestor.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: chopper
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: Wait, what if one scout IS an ancestor of the other, like p=2 and q=4? Does the trick still work?

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: robin
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: Yes — at post 2, 4 wouldn't be strictly less than 2, so we'd stop right there and 2 itself is the split point, since 2 is an ancestor of 4 by definition.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: luffy
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: SHISHISHI! And we never even looked at 0, 8, 7, or 9 — straight down the middle and stop the second the trail forks!

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.speaker: zoro
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-bst.line: One pass, no stored paths. That's the whole trick.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: usopp
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: Sengoku's vault is sorted like a search tree: k = 3. We need the 3rd-smallest bounty on file — without dumping every entry out and sorting them again.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: robin
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: Here's the trick: an INORDER walk — all of the left branch, then the node itself, then all of the right branch — visits every BST node in strictly ascending order automatically. That's just what left-smaller/right-bigger means, applied recursively.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: nami
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: So instead of pulling all six values into a list and sorting — O(n log n) — we walk in that order and just COUNT, stopping the instant we hit the k-th one.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: robin
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: Dive left first, as far as it goes: 5 to 3.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: robin
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: Still left: 2 to 1 — no left child left. This is the smallest value in the whole tree, so the walk finally visits it first.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: robin
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: Visit 1: count = 1. Not k yet. 1 has no right child either, so step back up to 2.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: robin
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: Visit 2: count = 2. Still not k. No right child under 2 either, so step back up to 3.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: robin
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: Visit 3: count = 3. That's k! The 3rd smallest value is 3 — stop right here.

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: luffy
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: SHISHISHI! And we never even had to look at 4, 5, or 6!

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.speaker: chopper
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.line: That early stop matters a lot when k is small but the vault has thousands of entries — no reason to sort the whole thing.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: usopp
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Weatheria's tracking six storm readings: 3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4. We need the SECOND-strongest one, k = 2.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: nami
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Sort all six and grab index 1 — that works, but it's O(n log n) and we'd re-sort readings we don't even care about.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Instead, keep a min-heap that holds only the k STRONGEST readings seen so far. Every time it grows past size k, evict the weakest one inside it. Whatever survives on top at the end is exactly the k-th largest.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Reading 3: heap has room, just drop it in.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Reading 2: heap has room (size 2 = k), drop it in too. Weakest on top is 2.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Reading 1: push it in, now size 3 — over capacity. Evict the weakest, which is 1 itself.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Reading 5: push, size 3, evict weakest — that's 2 this time.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Reading 6: push, size 3, evict weakest — 3 goes.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: Reading 4: push, size 3, evict weakest — 4 is the smallest of the three, so it's the one that leaves immediately.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: robin
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: No readings left. The weakest survivor still sitting on top of our size-2 heap is 5 — that's the 2nd-strongest storm.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: luffy
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: SHISHISHI! We only ever kept two readings around at once, no matter how many storms rolled in!

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.speaker: zoro
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.line: That's the point — the heap never holds more than k.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: usopp
top-k-frequent-elements.line: The Marines seized six wanted posters: 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3. HQ only has wall space for the k = 2 most REPRINTED faces.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: nami
top-k-frequent-elements.line: First, count how often each face shows up — that part's unavoidable. Tally: 1 appears 3 times, 2 appears 2 times, 3 appears once.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: robin
top-k-frequent-elements.line: Now, instead of sorting all the distinct faces by count, keep a min-heap of size k keyed on frequency — the k MOST reprinted faces seen so far. Whenever it grows past k, evict whichever one has the LOWEST count.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: robin
top-k-frequent-elements.line: Face 1, count 3: heap has room, drop it in.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: robin
top-k-frequent-elements.line: Face 2, count 2: heap has room (size 2 = k), drop it in too. Lowest count on top is 2.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: robin
top-k-frequent-elements.line: Face 3, count 1: push it, size 3 — over capacity. Evict the lowest count in the heap, which is face 3 itself.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: robin
top-k-frequent-elements.line: No faces left to check. Whatever survives in the heap IS our answer, in any order.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: luffy
top-k-frequent-elements.line: SHISHISHI! Poor guy with just one poster never even had a chance!

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: chopper
top-k-frequent-elements.line: And notice we only ever compare COUNTS in the heap, not the face values — the values just ride along as the payload.

top-k-frequent-elements.speaker: zoro
top-k-frequent-elements.line: Two posters on the wall. Order doesn't matter, only the set does.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: usopp
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Three separate route maps, each already sorted by distance: [1,4,5], [1,3,4], and [2,6]. The Log Pose needs them merged into ONE single sorted route.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: nami
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Dump every stop into one pile and sort it fresh — that works, but it throws away the fact that each map is already sorted.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Instead: a min-heap holding just the CURRENT front stop of each map. Always take the smallest front overall, then bring that same map's next stop up to take its place.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Fronts loaded: map A shows 1, map B shows 1, map C shows 2.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Smallest is 1 from map A. Take it, output grows. Map A's next stop, 4, enters the heap.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Smallest is 1 from map B. Take it. Map B's next, 3, enters.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Smallest is 2 from map C. Take it. Map C's next, 6, enters.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Smallest is 3 from map B. Take it. Map B's next, 4, enters.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Two fronts tied at 4 — take either, say map A's. It has no stops left after this.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Take map B's 4. Map B is now fully exhausted — nothing new enters for it.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: robin
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: Take 6, map C's last stop. Heap's empty — every map is fully spent.

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: luffy
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: SHISHISHI! One clean route out of three tangled ones!

merge-k-sorted-lists.speaker: zoro
merge-k-sorted-lists.line: The heap only ever holds one stop per map — never more than k at a time.

subsets.speaker: usopp
subsets.line: Three treasure emblems on the table: 1, 2, 3. The archivists want every possible bag we could pack — including the empty bag and the bag with everything.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: For each emblem, in order, we either pack it or skip it. And here's the key: EVERY partial bag we build along the way is itself a valid answer — not just the finished ones. Record it, then keep going deeper.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: Bag is empty right now — that counts as subset #1 all by itself.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: Pack 1. New bag [1] — record it, subset #2.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: Pack 2 on top of 1. Bag [1,2] — subset #3.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: Pack 3 on top. Bag [1,2,3] — subset #4. Nothing left after 3, so UNDO it — unpack 3, unpack 2 — back to just [1].

subsets.speaker: nami
subsets.line: That's the backtrack — we don't restart from scratch, we just take the last item back out and try the NEXT option in its place.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: With 1 still packed, try 3 instead of 2. Bag [1,3] — subset #5. Fully unpack back to empty.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: Bag empty again. Skip 1 entirely, pack 2. Bag [2] — subset #6. Pack 3 too: [2,3] — subset #7. Unpack both.

subsets.speaker: robin
subsets.line: Only 3 left to try alone: bag [3] — subset #8. Unpack it — nothing left to try anywhere.

subsets.speaker: luffy
subsets.line: SHISHISHI! Eight bags out of three emblems — empty, three singles, three pairs, and the full haul!

subsets.speaker: zoro
subsets.line: Two choices per emblem, three emblems: 2×2×2 = 8. Every combo, exactly once.

permutations.speaker: usopp
permutations.line: Three crewmates — 1, 2, 3 — need a lookout ORDER for the Crow's Nest. Unlike splitting loot, order matters here: [1,2,3] and [3,2,1] are different rotations.

permutations.speaker: robin
permutations.line: Fill the rotation one slot at a time: pick any crewmate not already placed, add them to the line, and recurse. Once the line is full, record it — then undo the last pick and try the next unused crewmate in that slot.

permutations.speaker: robin
permutations.line: Slot 2: only 2 or 3 remain unused — place 2.

permutations.speaker: robin
permutations.line: Slot 3: only 3 remains — place it. Line is full: [1,2,3] — rotation #1!

permutations.speaker: robin
permutations.line: Undo slot 3, undo slot 2 — back to [1]. Now try 3 in slot 2 instead, since 2 was already tried there.

permutations.speaker: robin
permutations.line: Slot 2: place 3. Slot 3: only 2 left — place it. [1,3,2] — rotation #2! Then fully undo back to an empty line.

permutations.speaker: nami
permutations.line: Every branch under '1 first' is used up — two rotations found. Same process starting with 2 first, then with 3 first.

permutations.speaker: usopp
permutations.line: Starting with 2: [2,1,3] and [2,3,1]. Starting with 3: [3,1,2] and [3,2,1]. Same choose-recurse-undo dance, every time.

permutations.speaker: luffy
permutations.line: SHISHISHI! Six full rotations out of three lookouts!

permutations.speaker: zoro
permutations.line: 3 choices for slot 1, times 2 for slot 2, times 1 for slot 3 — 3! = 6. Order matters, so it grows a lot faster than subsets did.

combination-sum.speaker: usopp
combination-sum.line: Punk Hazard's toll gate takes coins worth 2, 3, 6, or 7 — reuse any coin as many times as you like — and it must land on EXACTLY 7. Find every way to pay it.

combination-sum.speaker: nami
combination-sum.line: Brute-forcing every possible pile of coins gets messy fast. Better: keep adding coins and track the running total, but the moment it goes OVER 7, that pile is dead — stop right there.

combination-sum.speaker: robin
combination-sum.line: And to avoid counting [2,3] and [3,2] as different piles, only add coins at the CURRENT position or later in the list — never go back to an earlier coin type.

combination-sum.speaker: robin
combination-sum.line: Add a third 2. Pile [2,2,2], sum 6. Still under 7 — keep going.

combination-sum.speaker: robin
combination-sum.line: A fourth 2 would push the sum to 8 — over target. Dead end, undo it. Try 3 instead: pile [2,2,3], sum 7. EXACT MATCH — pay accepted!

combination-sum.speaker: robin
combination-sum.line: Undo all the way back to empty. Try starting with 3 alone, or 6 alone — 3+3+3=9, 3+6=9, 6+6=12, 6+7=13 — every path through them overshoots 7 without ever landing on it exactly. All dead ends.

combination-sum.speaker: robin
combination-sum.line: Last candidate: 7 alone. Pile [7], sum 7 — EXACT MATCH again!

combination-sum.speaker: luffy
combination-sum.line: SHISHISHI! Two ways through the gate: three coins, or just the one big 7!

combination-sum.speaker: chopper
combination-sum.line: Pruning the moment the sum passes 7 is what saves us — no point building a taller pile once it's already too heavy.

number-of-islands.speaker: usopp
number-of-islands.line: The great cartographer Usopp presents: the reef chart, redrawn after last night's storm! Every square is either dry land or open sea — but the storm scrambled the shoreline into pieces!

number-of-islands.speaker: chopper
number-of-islands.line: But some land squares are right next to each other! Do we count each square as its own tiny island, or do the touching ones count as one big island?

number-of-islands.speaker: robin
number-of-islands.line: One big island — but only if they touch up, down, left, or right. Diagonal doesn't count. So here's the plan: scan every square once. The moment we find land we haven't charted yet, we flood outward from it, marking every connected land square as the SAME island before we move on.

number-of-islands.speaker: robin
number-of-islands.line: Top-left corner, (0,0): land, and unmarked. New island! I'll flood outward from here.

number-of-islands.speaker: robin
number-of-islands.line: Its right neighbor (0,1) is also land — same island, mark it too. Its neighbor below (0,1)'s square, (1,1), is land as well — still the same flood.

number-of-islands.speaker: robin
number-of-islands.line: No more unmarked land touches this cluster. That flood is finished — three squares, one island.

number-of-islands.speaker: usopp
number-of-islands.line: Keep scanning! (0,2) sea, skip. (1,0) sea, skip. (1,2) sea, skip. (2,0) and (2,1), both sea, skip and skip!

number-of-islands.speaker: chopper
number-of-islands.line: Bottom-right corner, (2,2) — land, and it's never been touched by either flood! That has to be a brand new island, all on its own.

number-of-islands.speaker: robin
number-of-islands.line: Correct — nothing connects to it. Its own island.

number-of-islands.speaker: luffy
number-of-islands.line: SHISHISHI! Two islands, and we only ever floated over each square ONE time! Perfect for exploring — and for lunch, if either one has meat!

number-of-islands.speaker: zoro
number-of-islands.line: Scan once. Flood once per new landmass. Never re-check a square that's already been claimed. That's the whole technique.

course-schedule.speaker: zoro
course-schedule.line: Four trials to earn passage past this island. The scroll says Trial 1 needs Trial 0 done first, Trial 2 also needs Trial 0, and Trial 3 needs BOTH Trial 1 and Trial 2 finished before you can attempt it. Can we even complete all four, or is there a loop trapping us?

course-schedule.speaker: robin
course-schedule.line: Count how many un-cleared prerequisites each trial still has — its 'in-degree'. Trial 0 needs none. Trial 1 and Trial 2 each need one. Trial 3 needs two. Any trial with zero remaining prerequisites can be attempted right now.

course-schedule.speaker: robin
course-schedule.line: Only Trial 0 starts at zero, so it goes in the queue first.

course-schedule.speaker: nami
course-schedule.line: Clear Trial 0. Now anything that listed Trial 0 as a prerequisite gets to drop it — Trial 1's count goes from 1 to 0, Trial 2's count goes from 1 to 0. Both join the queue.

course-schedule.speaker: nami
course-schedule.line: Clear Trial 1. Trial 3 needed it — drop Trial 3's count from 2 to 1. Not zero yet, so Trial 3 still waits.

course-schedule.speaker: nami
course-schedule.line: Clear Trial 2. Trial 3 needed this one too — drop its count from 1 to 0. NOW Trial 3 joins the queue.

course-schedule.speaker: nami
course-schedule.line: Clear Trial 3. Nothing left waiting on anything.

course-schedule.speaker: robin
course-schedule.line: We cleared all four trials — exactly numCourses of them. If even one trial had been stuck forever above zero, that would mean a cycle: two trials each secretly waiting on the other. But nothing got stuck here.

course-schedule.speaker: luffy
course-schedule.line: SHISHISHI! No loop, no trap — just do them in this order: 0, then 1 and 2 in either order, then 3!

course-schedule.speaker: zoro
course-schedule.line: Peel off whatever's free right now, update what depended on it, repeat. If the queue ever runs dry before every trial is cleared, it's impossible.

number-of-provinces.speaker: nami
number-of-provinces.line: Big Mom's tea table has a seat for every city on this stretch of the Grand Line. The invitation list tells us which pairs of cities have a DIRECT road between them. We need to know: how many separate travel clusters are there in total?

number-of-provinces.speaker: robin
number-of-provinces.line: Give every city its own little flag at first — each one is its own boss, its own root. Whenever we find a direct road between two cities, we merge their flags: one root absorbs the other. At the end, we just count how many distinct roots survive.

number-of-provinces.speaker: robin
number-of-provinces.line: City 0 and City 1: isConnected says 1 — a direct road. Merge them. City 1's flag now points to City 0's root.

number-of-provinces.speaker: nami
number-of-provinces.line: City 0 and City 2: isConnected says 0. No road, no merge — leave them as they are.

number-of-provinces.speaker: nami
number-of-provinces.line: City 1 and City 2: also 0. Still no road. City 2 stays its own boss the whole time.

number-of-provinces.speaker: robin
number-of-provinces.line: That's every pair checked. City 0 and City 1 share one root now; City 2 kept its own. Two distinct roots on the table.

number-of-provinces.speaker: luffy
number-of-provinces.line: SHISHISHI! Two tea tables' worth of guests, then! One big one for City 0 and City 1, and a lonely little one for City 2!

number-of-provinces.speaker: zoro
number-of-provinces.line: Union what's directly linked, let the roots settle, count what's left standing. Never had to trace a single full path by hand.

climbing-stairs.speaker: chopper
climbing-stairs.line: Five steps up to the crow's nest! On every move I can hop up 1 step or 2 steps. How many different ways could I hop all the way to the top?

climbing-stairs.speaker: nami
climbing-stairs.line: If we try to list every possible sequence of hops by hand it balloons fast. Better to build the answer step by step, using the answers for SMALLER staircases we've already solved.

climbing-stairs.speaker: robin
climbing-stairs.line: Base cases first. Standing at step 0 already, doing nothing, counts as exactly 1 way. And there's exactly 1 way to reach step 1 — a single hop.

climbing-stairs.speaker: robin
climbing-stairs.line: Every step after that: you either hopped 1 from the step before it, or hopped 2 from the step before THAT. So ways(step) = ways(step-1) + ways(step-2).

climbing-stairs.speaker: robin
climbing-stairs.line: Step 5, the crow's nest itself: ways(4) + ways(3) = 5 + 3 = 8.

climbing-stairs.speaker: luffy
climbing-stairs.line: SHISHISHI! Eight different ways up, and we barely climbed at all — we just added two numbers, five times!

climbing-stairs.speaker: zoro
climbing-stairs.line: You never need the whole staircase in memory, either. Just the last two numbers.

house-robber.speaker: nami
house-robber.line: Five houses on this street, holding 2, 7, 9, 3, and 1 thousand Berries. Rob two houses that are next-door neighbors and both alarms scream at once. What's the most we can walk away with?

house-robber.speaker: robin
house-robber.line: The alarms won't allow that, captain. For each house, we only have two honest choices: skip it and keep whatever was best up to the house before it, or rob it and add its loot to whatever was best up to TWO houses before it.

house-robber.speaker: robin
house-robber.line: House 0: nothing before it to conflict with. Best so far: rob it. 2.

house-robber.speaker: robin
house-robber.line: House 1: rob it alone (7) beats keeping house 0's 2. Best so far: 7 — meaning house 0's plan gets replaced.

house-robber.speaker: robin
house-robber.line: House 2: skip it and keep 7, OR rob it plus whatever was best before house 1 (which was house 0's 2): 9 + 2 = 11. 11 beats 7 — rob houses 0 and 2.

house-robber.speaker: robin
house-robber.line: House 3: skip it, keep 11, OR rob it plus the best before house 2 (house 1's 7): 3 + 7 = 10. 11 still wins — skip house 3 entirely.

house-robber.speaker: robin
house-robber.line: House 4: skip it, keep 11, OR rob it plus the best before house 3 (still 11, from houses 0 and 2): 1 + 11 = 12. 12 wins!

house-robber.speaker: nami
house-robber.line: Houses 0, 2, and 4: 2 + 9 + 1 = 12,000 Berries, and not one pair of them is adjacent.

house-robber.speaker: zoro
house-robber.line: Every house only ever looks two decisions back. No need to keep the whole street in your head.

coin-change.speaker: nami
coin-change.line: We owe exactly 6 Berries for the Log Pose, and our coin pouch only has denominations of 1, 3, and 4. We can use as many of each as we like — what's the FEWEST coins that add up to exactly 6?

coin-change.speaker: chopper
coin-change.line: Trying every combination of coins sounds like it could take forever if the amount's big!

coin-change.speaker: robin
coin-change.line: So build it up instead. For every amount from 0 to 6, ask: for each coin no bigger than this amount, what's 1 plus the fewest coins needed for (amount minus that coin)? Take the smallest result. Amount 0 needs 0 coins — that's free.

coin-change.speaker: robin
coin-change.line: Amount 1: only coin 1 fits. 1 + ways(0) = 1 + 0 = 1 coin.

coin-change.speaker: robin
coin-change.line: Amount 2: only coin 1 fits (twice, effectively). 1 + ways(1) = 1 + 1 = 2 coins.

coin-change.speaker: robin
coin-change.line: Amount 3: coin 1 gives 1 + ways(2) = 3. But coin 3 gives 1 + ways(0) = 1. The smaller wins — just 1 coin.

coin-change.speaker: robin
coin-change.line: Amount 4: coin 1 gives 1 + ways(3) = 2. Coin 3 gives 1 + ways(1) = 2. Coin 4 gives 1 + ways(0) = 1. Smallest is 1 coin.

coin-change.speaker: robin
coin-change.line: Amount 5: coin 1 gives 1 + ways(4) = 2. Coin 3 gives 1 + ways(2) = 3. Coin 4 gives 1 + ways(1) = 2. Smallest is 2 coins.

coin-change.speaker: robin
coin-change.line: Amount 6, the target: coin 1 gives 1 + ways(5) = 3. Coin 3 gives 1 + ways(3) = 2. Coin 4 gives 1 + ways(2) = 3. Smallest is 2 coins — that's coin 3 twice.

coin-change.speaker: luffy
coin-change.line: SHISHISHI! Two coins, both threes, and we're set sail!

coin-change.speaker: zoro
coin-change.line: Every amount only ever needs the answers already sitting below it. Nothing gets recomputed twice.

jump-game.speaker: usopp
jump-game.line: Five stepping stones across the current, each one lets you leap forward AT MOST as many stones as its power number says. Starting on stone 0, can the great Captain Usopp reach stone 4, the goal?

jump-game.speaker: luffy
jump-game.line: Just jump as far as you can, every time, right?

jump-game.speaker: robin
jump-game.line: That instinct is almost right — let's make it precise. Walk the stones left to right and track the FARTHEST stone reachable so far. If we ever reach a stone that's beyond that farthest mark, we're stuck in the water. If the farthest mark ever reaches or passes the goal, we've made it.

jump-game.speaker: robin
jump-game.line: Stone 0, power 2: farthest reach becomes 0 + 2 = stone 2.

jump-game.speaker: robin
jump-game.line: Stone 1 is within reach (1 ≤ 2). Its power 3 pushes us to 1 + 3 = stone 4 — already the goal stone!

jump-game.speaker: robin
jump-game.line: Stone 2 is within reach (2 ≤ 4). Its power 1 only reaches stone 3 — no improvement, farthest stays 4.

jump-game.speaker: robin
jump-game.line: Stone 3 is within reach (3 ≤ 4). Its power 1 only reaches stone 4 — still no improvement, farthest stays 4.

jump-game.speaker: robin
jump-game.line: Stone 4, the goal itself, is within reach (4 ≤ 4). We're done — it's reachable!

jump-game.speaker: luffy
jump-game.line: SHISHISHI! Fishman Island, here we come! And we didn't even need to know the EXACT path, just that we could always reach far enough!

jump-game.speaker: zoro
jump-game.line: One pass, one running number. The second the current position outruns the farthest reach, stop — it's over.

gas-station.speaker: nami
gas-station.line: Five gas stations on this ring route. Each one gives you a set amount of fuel, but it costs a set amount to reach the NEXT station. We start with an empty tank — which station lets us make the full loop without ever running dry?

gas-station.speaker: luffy
gas-station.line: Just try starting at every station and see which one works?

gas-station.speaker: nami
gas-station.line: That works, but it is slow to redo the whole loop five separate times. Here's the shortcut: if total gas across the whole route is at least total cost, SOME starting station must work. Walk the loop once, tracking the running tank from a candidate start — the moment the tank goes negative, none of the stations we just tried could have been the true start, so move the candidate to the very next station and reset the tank to zero.

gas-station.speaker: nami
gas-station.line: Station 0: gain 1, spend 3, net -2. Tank drops to -2 — negative! Station 0 can't be the start. Try station 1 next, tank resets to 0.

gas-station.speaker: nami
gas-station.line: Station 1: gain 2, spend 4, net -2. Tank drops to -2 again — negative! Move on to station 2, reset.

gas-station.speaker: nami
gas-station.line: Station 2: gain 3, spend 5, net -2. Negative again! Move on to station 3, reset.

gas-station.speaker: nami
gas-station.line: Station 3: gain 4, spend 1, net +3. Tank climbs to 3 — positive, keep station 3 as our candidate and keep going.

gas-station.speaker: nami
gas-station.line: Station 4: gain 5, spend 2, net +3. Tank climbs to 6 — still positive, and that was the last station on the loop.

gas-station.speaker: robin
gas-station.line: Total gas (1+2+3+4+5 = 15) equals total cost (3+4+5+1+2 = 15), so a valid start does exist — and since the tank never went negative again after we locked in station 3, that's the one.

gas-station.speaker: luffy
gas-station.line: SHISHISHI! Fill up at station 3 and we sail the whole loop without running dry!

gas-station.speaker: zoro
gas-station.line: Every time the tank goes negative, it wasn't the driver's fault — it was every station tried so far. Wipe the slate, start fresh from the next one.

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: usopp
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: Behold! Four balloons strung along the wall, each spanning a range: 10-to-16, 2-to-8, 1-to-6, and 7-to-12. One arrow fired straight up at a single point bursts EVERY balloon whose range includes that point. What's the fewest arrows the great sniper Usopp needs to pop them all?

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: chopper
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: There are only four, but if there were hundreds, trying every possible arrow position sounds impossible!

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: robin
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: First, sort the balloons by where they END: 1–6, then 2–8, then 7–12, then 10–16. Now the rule is simple — always aim the next arrow at the END of the earliest still-unburst balloon. That one arrow is guaranteed to also burst every later balloon that starts before or at that point.

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: usopp
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: First balloon, 1–6: no arrow fired yet. Fire one at x = 6, its end point!

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: robin
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: Next, 2–8: it starts at 2, which is at or before our arrow at 6. Already burst — no new arrow needed.

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: usopp
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: Next, 7–12: it starts at 7, which is PAST our arrow at 6. This one survived! Fire a second arrow at its end, x = 12.

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: robin
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: Last, 10–16: it starts at 10, which is at or before our arrow at 12. Already burst by the same second arrow — no new arrow needed.

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: usopp
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: All four balloons down, and it only took TWO arrows! The legendary sniper strikes again!

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: luffy
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: SHISHISHI! Usopp, that was actually really cool!

min-arrows-burst-balloons.speaker: zoro
min-arrows-burst-balloons.line: Sort by where things end, not where they start. Fire only when forced to. Nothing fancier than that.

single-number.speaker: usopp
single-number.line: Gather round! Five cards on the table at the Sabaody duel ring — and legend says every card has an exact rival among the others, matched down to the bit... except ONE, the Unrivaled Card!

single-number.speaker: nami
single-number.line: With only five cards I could just compare every pair by hand, sure. But if this table had ten thousand cards, checking every pair against every other one would take forever.

single-number.speaker: robin
