single-number.line: There is an operation called XOR — it flips a bit where two numbers disagree and leaves it alone where they agree. Two identical numbers XORed together always land on zero.

single-number.speaker: robin
single-number.line: And zero XORed with anything just gives back that thing unchanged. So if I fold every card into one running total, any card with a rival cancels itself out completely — no matter what order they arrive in.

single-number.speaker: robin
single-number.line: Card one: 4. Running total was 000, now it is 100.

single-number.speaker: robin
single-number.line: Card two: 1. Fold it in — total becomes 101.

single-number.speaker: robin
single-number.line: Card four: 1 again — its rival! Folding it in flips those same bits right back, so the 1's worth cancels out of the total. New total: 110.

single-number.speaker: robin
single-number.line: Card five: 2 again — its rival too! Cancel it out the same way. New total: 100.

single-number.speaker: zoro
single-number.line: Every card that had a partner is gone from the total now. Only the 4 never got cancelled.

single-number.speaker: luffy
single-number.line: SHISHISHI! The Unrivaled Card is the 4! We never even had to write down which cards we'd already seen!

single-number.speaker: robin
single-number.line: That is the whole trick: one running XOR, one pass, no lookup table needed — the pairs cancel themselves out of existence.

number-of-1-bits.speaker: chopper
number-of-1-bits.line: n is 1011 in binary — eleven! But how many of these four lanterns are actually lit? I only count two hands' worth of fingers, this is confusing!

number-of-1-bits.speaker: nami
number-of-1-bits.line: We could just walk through all 32 bit positions of a real integer and check each one for a 1. It works, but most positions are already zero — that is a lot of wasted checks.

number-of-1-bits.speaker: robin
number-of-1-bits.line: There is a shortcut: n AND (n minus 1) always clears out the lowest lit lantern and leaves every other lantern exactly as it was. So we just repeat that and count each clear, until n goes dark.

number-of-1-bits.speaker: robin
number-of-1-bits.line: n is 1011, n-1 is 1010. AND them together and the lowest lantern — bit0 — snuffs out. Count: 1.

number-of-1-bits.speaker: robin
number-of-1-bits.line: n is now 1010, n-1 is 1001. AND them and the next lowest lantern — bit1 — snuffs out. Count: 2.

number-of-1-bits.speaker: robin
number-of-1-bits.line: n is now 1000, n-1 is 0111. AND them and the last lantern — bit3 — snuffs out too. Count: 3.

number-of-1-bits.speaker: zoro
number-of-1-bits.line: n's zero now. Nothing left to clear. Three lanterns were lit.

number-of-1-bits.speaker: luffy
number-of-1-bits.line: SHISHISHI! Three! And that middle lantern was never even lit, we didn't waste a single check on it!

number-of-1-bits.speaker: robin
number-of-1-bits.line: That is the whole idea: only spend a step for each 1-bit that actually exists, instead of marching past every position whether it is lit or not.

counting-bits.speaker: brook
counting-bits.line: Yohohoho~ We need the bit-count for every number from 0 to 5 — though I have no bits to count myself, being all bone! Shall we compute each one fresh, from nothing?

counting-bits.speaker: nami
counting-bits.line: Recomputing from scratch for every single number is wasteful — we would be redoing work we basically already did for a smaller number just a few steps ago.

counting-bits.speaker: robin
counting-bits.line: Every number i is just its own half — i shifted right by one bit — with maybe one extra 1-bit tacked on at the end. So bits[i] = bits[i >> 1] + (i's last bit). We only ever look back at answers we already have.

counting-bits.speaker: robin
counting-bits.line: i=0 is our base case: zero has no bits at all. bits[0] = 0.

counting-bits.speaker: robin
counting-bits.line: i=1: half of 1 is 0, and 1's last bit is 1. bits[1] = bits[0] + 1 = 1.

counting-bits.speaker: robin
counting-bits.line: i=2: half of 2 is 1, and 2's last bit is 0. bits[2] = bits[1] + 0 = 1.

counting-bits.speaker: robin
counting-bits.line: i=3: half of 3 is 1, and 3's last bit is 1. bits[3] = bits[1] + 1 = 2.

counting-bits.speaker: robin
counting-bits.line: i=4: half of 4 is 2, and 4's last bit is 0. bits[4] = bits[2] + 0 = 1.

counting-bits.speaker: robin
counting-bits.line: i=5: half of 5 is 2, and 5's last bit is 1. bits[5] = bits[2] + 1 = 2.

counting-bits.speaker: zoro
counting-bits.line: 0, 1, 1, 2, 1, 2. Every answer built off one we already had.

counting-bits.speaker: brook
counting-bits.line: How elegant — not a single number computed twice from scratch! A tidy tune, wouldn't you agree?

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: usopp
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: Watch this, I'm building a scroll-tree! One node per letter, growing down from a single root — so any word that starts the same way shares the same trunk!

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: chopper
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: How do you even put a whole word like 'apple' into it?

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: robin
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: Letter by letter. Starting at the root, for each letter I either follow an existing branch or grow a brand new one — then I step down onto it before reading the next letter.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: robin
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: a-p-p-l-e: five new branches grow straight down from the root. The very last one gets marked as a true word ending, not just a passing letter.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: robin
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: search('apple') just retraces that same path. Every letter exists, and the final node is flagged as a word ending — so it returns true.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: nami
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: Now try search('app'). The path a-p-p exists — we can walk it fine — but that second 'p' node was only ever a pass-through on the way to 'apple'. It was never flagged as an ending.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: robin
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: So search('app') returns false. Existing on the path is not enough — the node has to be specifically marked as somebody's actual word ending.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: luffy
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: But what if I just want to know if ANY word starts with 'app', I don't care if 'app' itself is a whole word?

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: robin
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: That is startsWith — same walk, but we skip the end-flag check entirely. The path a-p-p exists, so startsWith('app') returns true, even though search('app') just told us false.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: usopp
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: Now insert('app')! No new branches needed at all — that second 'p' node already exists, we just raise its ending flag.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: zoro
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: search('app') returns true now. Same tree, one flag flipped.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.speaker: robin
implement-trie-prefix-tree.line: That is a trie in full: shared trunks for shared prefixes, and a single flag per node deciding whether that exact point is a real word.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: brook
design-add-and-search-words.line: Yohoho~ Three wanted posters go up on the same board tonight: bad, dad, mad. All rhyme, all three letters, all end the same way!

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: robin
design-add-and-search-words.line: Same trie idea as always — each word gets its own branch from the root, and the very last letter of each gets flagged as a true ending.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: nami
design-add-and-search-words.line: Someone asks for 'pad'. With three posters up that is nothing, but imagine thousands — we do not want to compare 'pad' letter-by-letter against every single stored word by hand.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: robin
design-add-and-search-words.line: We do not have to. At the root we look for a branch labeled 'p' — there is none. Dead end immediately, no letters left to check.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: zoro
design-add-and-search-words.line: search('bad') is the easy case. Walk b, a, d — every letter matches a real branch, and the last one is flagged. True.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: luffy
design-add-and-search-words.line: Now the weird one — '.ad'. That dot could be ANYTHING! How do you follow a branch that isn't even a real letter?

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: robin
design-add-and-search-words.line: You do not follow one branch — you try all of them. At a '.', branch out into every child the current node has, and keep searching down each one. If even ONE of those paths finishes as a match, the whole search is true.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: robin
design-add-and-search-words.line: '.ad': at the root the dot tries b, d, and m all at once. Following 'b' first: then 'a' matches for real, then 'd' matches and it is flagged. Found a match — stop right there.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: nami
design-add-and-search-words.line: And 'b..'? First letter is fixed as 'b', no wildcard there. But the next two are both dots — so from the 'b' branch, try every child at each level.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: robin
design-add-and-search-words.line: From 'b', the only child is 'a' — the first dot matches it. From there, the only child is the flagged 'd' — the second dot matches that too. True.

design-add-and-search-words.speaker: zoro
design-add-and-search-words.line: Every dot just means: branch into all children here instead of one.

replace-words.speaker: luffy
replace-words.line: The newspaper's full of huge fancy words — cattle, rattled, battery! Let's shrink every last one of 'em down to its root!

replace-words.speaker: usopp
replace-words.line: First we plant the roots in a scroll-tree: cat, bat, rat. Three branches from one trunk, each one flagged where the root word ends.

replace-words.speaker: robin
replace-words.line: Now for every word in the sentence, we walk it letter by letter down this tree. The instant we hit a flagged ending, we stop and swap in that root — we do not even look at the rest of the word.

replace-words.speaker: robin
replace-words.line: 'the': first letter 't' is not even a branch at the root. No match at all — it stays exactly as 'the'.

replace-words.speaker: robin
replace-words.line: 'cattle': c-a-t reaches a flagged ending after only three letters. We stop right there, ignoring 't', 'l', 'e' — swap in 'cat'.

replace-words.speaker: nami
replace-words.line: That is the key part: stop at the FIRST flagged root you reach, the shortest one. We never need to keep matching to the end of the full word.

replace-words.speaker: robin
replace-words.line: 'was': 'w' is not a branch at the root either. Stays as-is.

replace-words.speaker: robin
replace-words.line: 'rattled': r-a-t reaches a flagged ending. Stop, ignore 't', 'l', 'e', 'd' — swap in 'rat'.

replace-words.speaker: robin
replace-words.line: 'by' and 'the' again: neither one even starts down a branch that exists. Both stay unchanged.

replace-words.speaker: robin
replace-words.line: 'battery': b-a-t reaches a flagged ending. Stop, ignore 't', 'e', 'r', 'y' — swap in 'bat'.

replace-words.speaker: zoro
replace-words.line: 'the cat was rat by the bat.' Every long word traded for its shortest root.

replace-words.speaker: luffy
replace-words.line: SHISHISHI! Way faster than reading the whole big word every time!

group-anagrams.speaker: usopp
group-anagrams.line: Six wanted-poster scrolls came off the same press, but the ink smudged and the letters got shuffled on half of them — eat, tea, tan, ate, nat, bat. Some of these are the SAME name in disguise!

group-anagrams.speaker: chopper
group-anagrams.line: How do we know which ones are really the same name spelled differently, without checking every scroll against every other scroll?

group-anagrams.speaker: nami
group-anagrams.line: We don't compare scroll to scroll at all. Sort the letters INSIDE each name alphabetically first — two names that are anagrams of each other always collapse to the exact same sorted spelling. That sorted spelling becomes the label on a filing box.

group-anagrams.speaker: nami
group-anagrams.line: "eat" sorted is "a-e-t". No box with that label yet — open a new one.

group-anagrams.speaker: nami
group-anagrams.line: "tea" sorted is also "a-e-t" — straight into the same box as "eat".

group-anagrams.speaker: nami
group-anagrams.line: "tan" sorted is "a-n-t" — a new label, a new box.

group-anagrams.speaker: nami
group-anagrams.line: "bat" sorted is "a-b-t" — nothing matches. A third box.

group-anagrams.speaker: zoro
group-anagrams.line: Three boxes. One pass. Never opened a scroll twice.

group-anagrams.speaker: luffy
group-anagrams.line: SHISHISHI! Same crew, different aliases — can't fool a filing box! So, is any of these boxes food?

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: usopp
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: The Log Pose lists six island coordinates in whatever order the current dropped them in — 100, 4, 200, 1, 3, 2 — and somewhere in that mess is the longest unbroken CHAIN of islands, each exactly one degree east of the last!

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: luffy
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Easy! Just line them up smallest to biggest and walk the line!

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: robin
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Sorting works, Luffy, but it costs extra time just to line them up. There's a faster way: drop every coordinate into a set for instant checking, then only START walking a chain from an island that has NO island directly to its west — because that means nothing shorter completes it. It has to be a true beginning.

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: robin
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Island 100 — is 99 anywhere on the list? No. So 100 begins a chain. Walk east: is 101 on the list? No. Chain length one.

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: robin
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Island 4 — is 3 on the list? Yes. Then 4 can never be a chain's start, only its middle — skip it without walking anywhere.

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: robin
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Island 200 — is 199 on the list? No, another true start. Walk east: 201 isn't there either. Chain length one, no better than before.

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: robin
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Island 1 — is 0 on the list? No! A true start. Walk east: 2 is there, 3 is there, 4 is there, 5 is not. That chain runs four islands deep.

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: robin
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Island 3 and island 2 each have a western neighbor already on the list, so they were never chain starts at all — just the middle of the chain we already counted, from island 1.

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: zoro
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: Four islands, one look each. That's the longest run: 1, 2, 3, 4.

longest-consecutive-sequence.speaker: luffy
longest-consecutive-sequence.line: SHISHISHI! And we never even had to sort the map!

container-with-most-water.speaker: zoro
container-with-most-water.line: Nine stone pillars, all different heights. Wall off two of them and the gap between holds rainwater — but only up to whichever pillar is SHORTER. Height above that just spills over the top.

container-with-most-water.speaker: nami
container-with-most-water.line: Checking every pair by hand is thirty-six comparisons for just nine pillars. That's not a plan, that's a chore.

container-with-most-water.speaker: chopper
container-with-most-water.line: Is there a way to skip pairs without even looking at them?

container-with-most-water.speaker: robin
container-with-most-water.line: Start at the two outer pillars — the widest gap there is. Whichever one is shorter is the bottleneck. Moving the TALLER pillar's pointer inward only shrinks the width and can never raise that bottleneck, so it can never help. Only stepping the SHORTER pillar inward has any chance of finding something taller. That's the only move we ever make.

container-with-most-water.speaker: robin
container-with-most-water.line: Pillar 0 is height 1, pillar 8 is height 7. Width eight, bottleneck height one — eight units of water. The left pillar is shorter, so it steps in.

container-with-most-water.speaker: nami
container-with-most-water.line: Pillar 1 is height 8, pillar 8 is still height 7. Width seven, bottleneck height seven — forty-nine! Now the RIGHT pillar is the shorter one, so it steps in next.

container-with-most-water.speaker: robin
container-with-most-water.line: From here the gap only keeps narrowing — pillar 8 down to 7, then 6, then 5, then 4, then 3, then 2 — and none of those widths, even against pillar 1's wall of eight, can multiply back up to forty-nine.

container-with-most-water.speaker: chopper
container-with-most-water.line: So the two walls just keep marching toward each other until they meet, and whatever the biggest number we wrote down was — that stays the answer?

container-with-most-water.speaker: robin
container-with-most-water.line: Exactly. Once the pointers meet, we stop. Best area found: forty-nine, between pillar 1 and pillar 8.

container-with-most-water.speaker: zoro
container-with-most-water.line: One pass. Never move the tall one.

three-sum.speaker: brook
three-sum.line: Yohoho — imagine the ship's ledger as a row of six entries, already sorted from biggest debt to biggest credit: minus four, minus one, minus one, zero, one, two. Somewhere in here are THREE entries that balance to exactly zero, with no entry counted twice.

three-sum.speaker: chopper
three-sum.line: Do we have to check every possible group of three? With six entries that's already twenty different trios!

three-sum.speaker: nami
three-sum.line: Not one at a time. The ledger is sorted, so we fix ONE entry as the anchor, then hunt the other two with a two-pointer sweep on the rest — the exact same trick as balancing a two-person split, just repeated for every possible anchor.

three-sum.speaker: nami
three-sum.line: Anchor at minus four. Left pointer starts right after it, right pointer at the far end: minus one and two.

three-sum.speaker: nami
three-sum.line: Minus four plus minus one plus two is minus three — too low. Only sliding the left pointer up can raise that sum, and it never catches up before the pointers cross. No triplet starts with minus four.

three-sum.speaker: nami
three-sum.line: Anchor moves to the next entry: minus one. Left resets just after it, right resets to the far end.

three-sum.speaker: nami
three-sum.line: Minus one plus minus one plus two is zero — balanced! Log this trio, then slide both pointers inward to keep hunting under the same anchor.

three-sum.speaker: nami
three-sum.line: Left steps to zero, right steps to one: minus one plus zero plus one is zero again — a second balanced trio!

three-sum.speaker: zoro
three-sum.line: Next anchor candidate is another minus one — the same value we already anchored on. Skip it, or we'd just log the same two trios twice.

three-sum.speaker: chopper
three-sum.line: Anchor moves to zero: zero plus one plus two is three — too high, and the pointers are already meeting each other. Nothing there.

three-sum.speaker: brook
three-sum.line: Two trios, no repeats. The ledger balances to zero exactly twice. Yohoho!

minimum-window-substring.speaker: usopp
minimum-window-substring.line: This parchment reads A-D-O-B-E-C-O-D-E-B-A-N-C, and somewhere inside it is the SMALLEST possible stretch that still holds one A, one B, and one C. Find it, and only it gets copied out!

minimum-window-substring.speaker: luffy
minimum-window-substring.line: Just find any place where A, B, and C are all sitting close together, right? Why does it matter if it's the SMALLEST one?

minimum-window-substring.speaker: nami
minimum-window-substring.line: Because every letter of parchment we copy costs us ink and time — the smaller the excerpt, the less we haul back to the ship.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: So: grow a window from the right until it holds every letter we need, then shrink it from the left as far as it'll still hold all of them, recording the smallest valid window each time. Expand to satisfy, shrink to tighten.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: Expanding right — A, D, O, B, E, C. The moment C lands, at index five, I finally have one A, one B, one C. First valid window: index zero through five.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: Try shrinking from the left — but dropping index zero throws away my only A. Invalid. So I stop shrinking there and keep pushing the right edge instead.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: O, D, E, B go by — nothing new needed — until index ten brings a second A. Valid again, but now the window stretches all the way from index one to index ten.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: Now shrink hard — D, O, that extra B, and E all peel off the left edge without losing A, B, or C, since I still have the C at index five and the new A at index ten.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: One more drop takes the C at index five with it — invalid. Stop shrinking, push right again.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: N goes by, then a second C lands at index twelve. Valid once more, window six through twelve.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: robin
minimum-window-substring.line: Shrink again — O, D, E, and that extra B all peel off, and I still hold my A, my B, my C. Index nine through twelve: B, A, N, C. Four letters — the smallest yet.

minimum-window-substring.speaker: nami
minimum-window-substring.line: Try to drop the B at index nine and the window loses its only B — invalid, and there's no more parchment to the right. Four letters is as small as it gets.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: brook
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: A banner sewn letter by letter — A, A, B, A, B, B, A. We want the longest stretch that could be repainted into ONE repeated letter, but the sewing budget only allows repainting k = 1 patch. Yohoho, threadbare economics!

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: luffy
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Why not just repaint the WHOLE banner into one letter?

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Because the budget is only one patch, Luffy — that's what makes it hard. Grow a window to the right, and track the highest count any single letter has reached inside it so far — call it the record. The window stays affordable exactly when its length minus that record is at most our budget, because that difference IS how many patches repainting would cost. Cross the budget, shrink one letter off the left, and check again.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: First letter, A. Record for A is one. Window length one, cost zero. Affordable — window grows.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Second letter, A again. Record climbs to two. Window length two, cost zero. Still affordable.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Third letter, B. The record stays at two — B hasn't caught up. Window length three, cost one. Exactly at budget — still affordable.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Fourth letter, A. Record climbs to three. Window length four, cost one. Still exactly at budget.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Fifth letter, B. Record still three. Window length is now five — index zero through four — cost five minus three is two. Over budget! Drop the leftmost letter, index zero. Window shrinks to indices one through four, length four, cost back to one.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Sixth letter, B. Record holds at three. Window stretches to indices one through five, length five, cost two — over budget again. Drop index one. Window shrinks to indices two through five, length four, cost one.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: robin
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Last letter, A. Record holds at three. Window stretches to indices two through six, length five, cost two — over budget once more, and the banner's out of letters after this. Drop index two. Window shrinks to indices three through six, length four, cost one — that's as far as we get.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: zoro
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: Four letters, one patch, no better window left to check. That's the answer.

longest-repeating-character-replacement.speaker: luffy
longest-repeating-character-replacement.line: SHISHISHI! One patch of paint and we get a banner four letters strong. Iron Pirate Luffy approves!

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: usopp
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: Four scrolls on this treasure trail: three, then two, then zero, then negative four. But the last one doesn't lead off the map — it loops back into the trail somewhere earlier! Last time we only needed to know IF a trail loops. This time the Log Pose needs the EXACT scroll where the loop begins.

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: chopper
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: Same trick as the Florian Triangle — I walk one scroll at a time, you go Gear Second, two scrolls at a time. When we land on the same scroll, we know it loops. But that meeting scroll won't be where the loop actually STARTS — not yet.

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: luffy
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: One step in — I'm already at scroll zero. You're only at scroll two.

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: chopper
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: Two steps in — I've caught up to scroll zero. And you... you're back at scroll two? The trail looped you right back around already!

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: luffy
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: Three steps — I'm at negative four. And... so are you! Same scroll!

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: robin
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: That meeting scroll isn't where the loop begins — it's just wherever you two happened to collide. Here's the fix: send one of you all the way back to the very first scroll, scroll three. Leave the other exactly where you met. Now both of you walk at the SAME plain, one-scroll-a-time pace.

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: chopper
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: Back at the start — scroll three. You stay put at negative four, even if your legs want to keep running.

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: luffy
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: One step each — you go from three to two. I go from negative four to... two! Same scroll again!

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: robin
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: And that's the proof — wherever you two meet on this second, equal-speed walk is exactly where the loop begins. Scroll two is the front door of the loop.

linked-list-cycle-ii.speaker: zoro
linked-list-cycle-ii.line: Straight line, three to two. Loop after that: two, zero, negative four — forever.

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: usopp
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: Five flags strung along the cliff path, marking the way to the buried Den Den Mushi — numbered one through five in order. We need to plant a marker at the exact MIDDLE flag. Only rule: no measuring the path twice.

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: chopper
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: Can't we just count all five flags, then walk to flag number three?

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: nami
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: That's counting the whole path once, then walking part of it again — two passes. There's a way to do it in ONE walk: send one of us at normal speed and one at double speed, together. When the fast walker runs out of path, the slow walker is standing exactly on the middle flag.

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: brook
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: Yohohoho, a race where the loser wins just by standing still in the middle! Though — skeleton, I have no stamina to spend either way!

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: nami
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: One step for the slow walker: flag two. Two steps for the fast one: flag three.

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: nami
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: Another step slow: flag three. Another double-step fast: through flag four, landing on flag five — the very last flag. No path left ahead of it.

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: zoro
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: Fast walker's out of road. Slow walker's on flag three. That's the middle.

middle-of-the-linked-list.speaker: luffy
middle-of-the-linked-list.line: SHISHISHI, and neither of us ever had to count anything first! We just walked until one of us ran out of path!

insert-interval.speaker: usopp
insert-interval.line: Two ships already have dock slots booked at Water 7: hours one to three, and hours six to nine. Now a third ship radios in wanting hours two to five — and it isn't on the schedule yet. Where does it fit?

insert-interval.speaker: nami
insert-interval.line: If it doesn't overlap anybody, we just slot it in wherever it belongs by start hour and we're done. But if it DOES overlap an existing booking, we can't just staple it in — the two bookings have to become one longer one.

insert-interval.speaker: robin
insert-interval.line: So we walk the schedule once, in order, and ask three questions about each existing slot: does it end before the new one even starts? Does it start after the new one ends? Or does it overlap?

insert-interval.speaker: robin
insert-interval.line: First slot, one to three. It doesn't end before hour two — its end, three, is past our new booking's start. And it doesn't start after hour five either. That's overlap. We swallow it into the new booking: the low end stays one, the high end stretches to five.

insert-interval.speaker: robin
insert-interval.line: Second slot, six to nine. Does it start after our now-grown booking ends at five? Yes — six is past five. No overlap. That means our merged booking is finished growing, and this slot stands entirely on its own, right after it.

insert-interval.speaker: nami
insert-interval.line: One to five, then six to nine. Two slots on the board, in order — and the new ship folded straight into slot one without anybody reshuffling the whole schedule by hand.

insert-interval.speaker: luffy
insert-interval.line: So the new ship didn't even need its OWN dock — it just moved into ship one's spot and made it bigger! SHISHISHI!

insert-interval.speaker: zoro
insert-interval.line: One pass. Keep what ends before you start, merge whatever overlaps, keep what starts after you end.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: usopp
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Four scouts radio in wanting the watchtower tonight — shift windows of one to four, two to three, three to five, and six to eight o'clock. Some of those windows overlap. We can only grant the shifts that never overlap each other, and I want as few scouts turned away as possible.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: usopp
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Feels natural to just grant whoever asked with the EARLIEST start first — scout one, one to four. Lock that in and work forward from there, right?

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: robin
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Not quite. Sort by when each shift ENDS instead of when it starts. The window that frees the tower soonest — two to three — should always be the one we lock in first, no matter who asked, or when.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: nami
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Two to three doesn't conflict with anything yet, so it's free — keep it. Tower's booked until hour three.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: nami
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Next shortest-to-end: scout one's one to four. But it STARTS at hour one — before hour three, when the tower's already booked. Overlap. Turn this one away.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: nami
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Next: three to five. Starts right at hour three, the moment the tower frees up — no overlap. Keep it. Booked until hour five now.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: usopp
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Last one: six to eight. Starts well after hour five. Clean. Keep it too.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: nami
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Three shifts kept, only one scout turned away. If I'd locked in scout one's LONG shift first like I almost suggested, it would have blocked both two-to-three AND three-to-five — we'd have kept fewer overall.

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: luffy
non-overlapping-intervals.line: So the shift that gets OUT OF THE WAY fastest wins the tower first, even if it asked last! SHISHISHI, that's just like leaving room for everyone at the dinner table!

non-overlapping-intervals.speaker: zoro
non-overlapping-intervals.line: Sort by end. Keep it if it starts on or after the last one you kept ends. Count what you drop.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: usopp
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: Okay, I MIGHT have tripped into the garden sundial this morning. The seven hour-stones used to run smallest to biggest all the way around — now they're spun to some unknown point. There's exactly one smallest stone hiding in the ring. Find it, and we know exactly how far it turned.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: chopper
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: Can't we just look at all seven stones and see which one is smallest?

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: nami
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: With seven, sure. With seven hundred, we'd be here all night. It's still HALF sorted at any split — same trick as always: cut it in half and figure out which half is hiding the smallest one.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: nami
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: Middle stone, index three: marked seven. Compare it to the LAST stone in range, index six, marked two. Seven is bigger than two — stones only ever drop from big back to small once, at the turnover point, and that hasn't happened yet by index three. So the smallest stone is still ahead, past the middle.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: nami
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: Throw away index zero through three entirely. Search only four through six.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: nami
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: New middle: index five, marked one. Compare to the last stone in THIS range, index six, marked two. One is smaller — the turnover already happened at or before index five, so the smallest is index five or something before it, never past it.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: nami
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: Search shrinks to index four through five.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: nami
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: Middle now: index four, marked zero. Compare to the last stone in range, index five, marked one. Zero is smaller — same logic. The smallest is index four or before, never past it.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: nami
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: That shrinks the range to exactly one stone: index four.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: robin
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: One stone left in range means we're done — a range of exactly one is always, trivially, its own smallest. Stone marked zero it is.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: luffy
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: SHISHISHI, so the sundial used to start counting right THERE, and Usopp spun it four stones too far!

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.speaker: zoro
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.line: Compare the middle to the edge of your range, not to some target. Bigger than the edge, the drop's still ahead — go right. Smaller or equal, the drop already happened — stay left, keep the middle in play.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: nami
koko-eating-bananas.line: Sunny casts off in exactly eight hours — the tide won't wait a minute longer. There are four banana piles left on the dock: three, six, seven, and eleven bananas. And Luffy will not leave without every single one eaten first.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: luffy
koko-eating-bananas.line: I'll eat every pile! Just tell me how many bananas an hour to go so it fits EXACTLY in eight hours — and not one banana an hour faster than I have to, my stomach has limits!

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: chopper
koko-eating-bananas.line: Remember the rule — you eat from only ONE pile per hour, at whatever speed you pick. If the pile runs out partway through the hour, that hour is still used up; you can't start a new pile until the next one begins.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: robin
koko-eating-bananas.line: So we aren't searching the piles at all — we're searching every POSSIBLE eating speed, from one banana an hour up to eleven, enough to clear the biggest pile in a single hour. Somewhere in that range is the slowest speed that still finishes on time. Binary search for it.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: chopper
koko-eating-bananas.line: Try the middle speed: six bananas an hour. Pile three needs one hour, pile six needs one hour, pile seven needs two hours, pile eleven needs two hours. Six hours total.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: robin
koko-eating-bananas.line: Six hours fits inside our eight-hour limit with room to spare, so speed six works — but something slower might work too. Keep searching the lower half.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: chopper
koko-eating-bananas.line: Middle of what's left: three bananas an hour. One hour, two hours, three hours, four hours — ten hours total.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: robin
koko-eating-bananas.line: Ten hours blows past our eight-hour limit. Speed three is too slow — Luffy needs to eat faster than that. Search the upper half instead.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: chopper
koko-eating-bananas.line: Middle of four to six: five bananas an hour. One hour, two hours, two hours, three hours — eight hours total.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: robin
koko-eating-bananas.line: Eight hours lands exactly on the limit — that still counts as finishing on time. Five works. Try slower still.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: chopper
koko-eating-bananas.line: Middle of four to five: four bananas an hour. One hour, two hours, two hours, three hours — eight hours total, again exactly at the limit.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: robin
koko-eating-bananas.line: Four also finishes exactly on time, and there's nothing slower left in range to test. Four bananas an hour is the floor.

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: luffy
koko-eating-bananas.line: Four it is! ...wait, that's SLOWER than I wanted to eat! Ugh — fine. SHISHISHI, still bananas!

koko-eating-bananas.speaker: zoro
koko-eating-bananas.line: We never searched the piles themselves — only the speeds. Same halving trick, aimed at a different range.
